food_subgroup: Stuffed vegetable dishes; olive-oil-based braised preparations

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Imam Bayildi. FOODON_03302765 (stuffed eggplant food product) is the closest defensible parent class covering whole aubergine stuffed with vegetables and simmered in olive oil.
ontology.foodex2_description: Aubergines (eggplants), prepared — closest FoodEx2 category for a cooked aubergine-based dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Imam Bayildi. A0FMT (aubergines/eggplants, prepared) is the nearest defensible parent. The dish also contains onion, garlic, tomato, and olive oil but the primary ingredient is aubergine.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Imam Bayildi or a sufficiently close stuffed-aubergine dish. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Imam Bayildi consists of one whole aubergine (or one large half) stuffed with the onion-tomato-garlic mixture, weighing approximately 250–350 g including the olive oil braising liquid.

nutrient_source.title: Imam Bayildi — Turkish stuffed aubergine dish; nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients (aubergine/eggplant cooked, onion cooked, tomato, garlic, olive oil) weighted by standard recipe proportions, cross-referenced with Turkish food composition data (Türkiye Gıda Kompozisyon Veri Tabanı, BeBiS reference values for patlıcan yemeği)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central; BeBiS / Turkish Food Composition Database (reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Imam Bayildi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient data for cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169229), cooked onion (FDC ID 170000), raw tomato (FDC ID 170457), garlic (FDC ID 169230), and olive oil (FDC ID 171413), combined using a standard recipe ratio (approx. 55% aubergine, 20% onion-tomato mixture, 20% olive oil, 5% garlic-parsley-salt). The resulting per-100 g values are consistent with published Turkish dietary reference values for olive-oil-braised aubergine dishes (patlıcan yemeği). Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of eggplant and olive-oil-braised vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked eggplant GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Imam Bayildi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the GI of cooked eggplant (GI ≈ 15, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly aubergine with olive oil, onion, tomato, and garlic — all low-GI ingredients. The high olive oil content further attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (15 × 6.8 × 3) / 100 = 3.1 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g); restaurant portions may contain significantly more added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, as restaurant versions of Imam Bayildi can contain 400–600 mg sodium per serving; the dish's high potassium content (~280 mg/100 g) from aubergine and tomato partially offsets sodium load and supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load (~3 per serving) due to aubergine and olive oil base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Imam Bayildi is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (≈15), high dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g), and predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil, which supports postprandial glucose control and insulin sensitivity; portion size should be monitored to limit total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat from olive oil (~5.6 g/100 g); zero cholesterol; rich in antioxidants from aubergine and tomato
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominance of monounsaturated fatty acids from extra-virgin olive oil, combined with zero dietary cholesterol and antioxidant compounds (nasunin in aubergine, lycopene in tomato), makes Imam Bayildi a cardioprotective choice consistent with Mediterranean dietary patterns; total fat intake (~7.8 g/100 g) should be considered in the context of overall daily fat targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Imam Bayildi (İmam Bayıldı) is a classic Ottoman and Turkish dish whose name translates literally as 'the imam fainted' — a reference to the legendary richness of the olive oil used in its preparation. The dish consists of whole aubergines (eggplants) that are slit lengthwise and stuffed generously with a slow-cooked mixture of onion, garlic, and tomato, then braised or simmered in abundant extra-virgin olive oil until the aubergine is completely tender and the filling is deeply caramelised. It is served at room temperature or slightly warm, making it suitable as a meze, a main vegetarian course, or a side dish. The texture is silky and yielding, with the aubergine flesh absorbing the olive oil and the sweet-savoury filling. The flavour profile is rich, slightly sweet from the slow-cooked onion and tomato, with a pronounced olive oil character and mild garlic depth. Nutritionally, Imam Bayildi is a plant-based, gluten-free, vegan dish with a very low glycemic index (approximately 15) and a low glycemic load per serving, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking heart-healthy Mediterranean-style meals. The dish provides dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g), potassium (280 mg/100 g), vitamin C, and vitamin E, alongside predominantly monounsaturated fats from olive oil. Sodium content is moderate and varies by preparation. In Singapore, Imam Bayildi is available at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay, typically priced around SGD 16.90 per portion.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Imam Bayildi as a composite dish in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked eggplant GI from the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are ingredient-level estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central; no exact composite-dish entry exists in any approved primary source for Imam Bayildi. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a validated composite-dish analysis becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as olive oil and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as plant-based ingredients contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), potassium_mg (280.0), calcium_mg (22.0), iron_mg (0.5), cholesterol_mg (0.0), vitamin_c_mg (8.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.04), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.6), vitamin_b6_mg (0.12), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.0), vitamin_e_mg (1.4), phosphorus_mg (38.0), magnesium_mg (14.0), zinc_mg (0.2), selenium_mcg (0.4), water_g (82.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient data; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates are available for all fields. 7. This is a Turkish dish; multilingual naming rules for Turkish cuisine do not mandate Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names — those fields are null as not applicable. The romanized_local field captures the authentic Turkish orthography (İmam Bayıldı). 8. field survey source data preserved: food_id GD000268, served by Sultan Palace at Boat Quay (2 listings), price SGD 16.90. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.8 × 3.0) / 100 = 3.1 g (300 g serving).
